summary: Graham argues that succinctness is essentially equivalent to power in programming languages, since the entire purpose of high-level languages is to compress what would require thousands of lines of machine code into fewer lines of source. He proposes measuring language power by counting conceptual elements rather than lines or characters, and contends that restrictiveness in a language is mostly the feeling of being forced to write longer programs than necessary.
key_points: Succinctness is the primary purpose of high-level languages — they exist to give leverage over machine code
The best metric for code size is the number of conceptual elements (tree nodes), not lines or characters
Brooks' finding that programmers produce similar amounts of code per day regardless of language implies more succinct languages yield faster development
Restrictiveness in a language is mostly the feeling of being forced to write longer programs than the ones in your head
Per-line readability can be a marketing advantage but a design disadvantage, since total reading effort equals effort-per-line times number of lines
money_quotes: It seems to me that succinctness is what programming languages are for.
The true test of a language is how well you can discover and solve new problems, not how well you can use it to solve a problem someone else has already formulated.
I think the way to find (or design) the best language is to become hypersensitive to how well a language lets you think, then choose/design the language that feels best.
Restrictiveness is mostly lack of succinctness.
Installment plans are a net lose for the buyer, though, as mere readability-per-line probably is for the programmer.
key_insight: The power of a programming language is best measured by how few conceptual elements it requires to express a program, because succinctness is not merely a nice property — it is the fundamental purpose of high-level languages.
